title: Rate Risk vs. Credit Risk — and the Spread

screens.heading: Two ways to lose money in a bond
screens.body: Everything so far has been rate risk: prices moving because the price of time moved. But a bond is also a promise from a specific borrower, and promises can weaken. Credit risk is the chance you are not paid in full and on time — and the market prices it separately, continuously, in its own channel.

screens.heading: Yield decomposed
screens.body: A corporate bond's yield is roughly the risk-free rate for that maturity PLUS a spread — compensation for this issuer's default risk, for bearing that risk, and for the bond's lesser liquidity. Rates move the base; credit moves the spread. These are the two first-order channels through which a bond's mark-to-market value changes. They are not the whole of what you earn: income and the drift of the price along the curve also contribute, which is the final lesson's subject.
screens.formula: Corporate yield ≈ risk-free rate + credit spread

screens.heading: The spread is a live price
screens.body: At its floor, a spread has to cover expected loss — the chance of default times how much is lost when it happens. But spreads are quotes, not formulas: they widen when the market turns fearful and grind tighter when it is calm, repricing trade by trade. Watching a bond's spread is watching the market's running opinion of one borrower's promise.

screens.heading: Losing 15 points without a default
screens.body: A rating agency downgrades a company deep into junk territory. The company hasn't missed a payment and may never miss one — but funds whose mandates require higher ratings are forced to sell, buyers demand a wider spread, and the bond drops 15 points. In traded credit, credit OPINIONS hit your price immediately; you don't have to wait for an actual default to lose money.

screens.heading: Two spreads that share a word

left.label: Loan spread (private credit)
left.body: Contractual: a margin over the floating base rate, negotiated at close and written into the loan agreement. It doesn't move with trading — it changes through an amendment, a repricing, or a pre-agreed pricing grid that steps the margin up or down with the borrower's leverage or rating.

right.label: Bond spread (traded markets)
right.body: Observed: backed out of the bond's market price against the government curve, and recomputed with every trade. It moves because the price moves — continuously, with no negotiation involved.

screens.verdict: Same borrower, same underlying default risk — but one number is set in a negotiation and adjusted by agreement, and the other floats on a market. The gap between them is information professionals trade.
